大正新脩大藏經
黃檗山斷際禪師傳心法要

大正新脩大藏經數位版, No. 2012A 黃檗山斷際禪師傳心法要
titleStmt.author: 唐 裴休集

distributor.name: 財團法人佛教電子佛典基金會 (CBETA)

availability.p: Available for non-commercial use when distributed with this header intact.

projectDesc.p: Text as provided by Mr. Hsiao Chen-Kuo, Text as provided by Electronic Buddhadharma Society (EBS), Text as provided by Anonymous from USA
蕭鎮國大德提供，佛教電腦資訊庫功德會提供，北美某大德提供

CW (ed.) Created initial TEI XML version with BASICX.BAT (00/01/24)

cb:div.cb:mulu: Essentials of Mind-Cutting at the Boundary (Preface)
cb:div.head: Essentials of Mind-Cutting at the Boundary
cb:div.byline: Collected and Prefaced by Pei Xiu of Hedong
cb:div.p:
There was a great Chan master, whose Dharma name was Xiyun, residing at Huangbo Mountain in Gao’an County of Hongzhou.
Beneath Vulture Peak. He was a direct Dharma descendant of the Sixth Patriarch of Caoxi and a Dharma nephew of Baizhang of Xitang.
He alone bore the seal of the supreme vehicle beyond words and letters, transmitting only the One Mind and nothing else.
There is no other Dharma. The essence of mind is empty and all conditions are utterly still, like the great sun rising in empty space.
In that space its radiance shines brilliantly, pure and without the slightest dust. Those who realize it have neither newness nor oldness, neither shallow nor deep.
Those who speak of it establish no doctrinal interpretations and set up no sectarian authority. Without opening doors or windows, it is directly so.
Immediately it is thus; the moment a thought moves, one deviates. Only then is the original Buddha realized. Therefore his words were concise.
His principle was direct, his Way lofty, and his conduct solitary. Students from the four directions gazed toward the mountain and hurried there.
Seeing him, many awakened. The assembly coming and going was always more than a thousand. In the second year of Huichang,
while serving as governor at Zhongling, I invited him from the mountain to the prefecture, where he stayed at Longxing Monastery, and morning and evening I asked about the Way.
In the second year of Dazhong, when I governed Wanling, I again respectfully invited him to my district, where he resided
at Kaiyuan Monastery. Morning and evening I received the Dharma. Afterwards I recorded it, though I captured scarcely one or two parts in ten, keeping it as
a seal of mind and not daring to circulate it. Now I fear that its subtle and profound meaning may not be heard in the future.
Therefore I brought it forth and entrusted it to the monk Fazhou of the Great Boat among my disciples, sending it back to Guangtang Monastery
on the old mountain, asking the elders and the assembly whether it agrees with what they had personally heard before,
or whether there are differences.
Preface written on the eighth day of the eleventh month in the eleventh year of the Tang Dazhong era.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: Essential Teachings on the Transmission of Mind by Chan Master Duanji of Huangbo Mountain

cb:div.p:
The master said to Xiu: All Buddhas and all sentient beings are only the One Mind; there is no other Dharma.
This mind since beginningless time has never been born and never perished. It is neither green nor yellow,
without form and without appearance. It does not belong to being or nonbeing, and cannot be reckoned as new or old. It is neither long nor
short, neither great nor small. It transcends all limits, names, words, traces, and oppositions.
It is precisely this very body. The moment a thought arises, one deviates. It is like empty space, without boundaries
and beyond measure. This One Mind alone is the Buddha. Between Buddha and sentient beings there is no difference.
Only sentient beings cling to appearances and seek outwardly; the more they seek, the more they lose. Making Buddha seek Buddha
and using mind to grasp mind, though they exhaust kalpas and lifetimes they will never obtain it. They do not know to stop thoughts
and forget concerns, whereupon the Buddha appears of itself. This mind is the Buddha, and the Buddha is sentient beings. When it is
a sentient being this mind does not diminish; when it is a Buddha this mind does not increase. Even the
six perfections, the myriad practices, and merits as numerous as the sands of the Ganges are originally complete within it and need not be cultivated to add anything.
When conditions arise they function; when conditions cease they are still. If one does not firmly believe that this is Buddha and instead wishes
to cling to appearances and cultivate practices seeking merit, all of this is delusion and contrary to the Way. This
mind is the Buddha. There is no other Buddha and no other mind. This mind is bright and pure,
like empty space without a single mark of form. The moment one raises a thought, one deviates from the Dharma body
and clings to appearances. Since beginningless time there has been no Buddha attached to appearances. To cultivate the six perfections and myriad practices wishing
to become a Buddha is a gradual method. Since beginningless time there has been no Buddha of stages. Simply awaken
to the One Mind and there is not the slightest Dharma to be attained. This is the true Buddha. Buddha and sentient beings are
one mind without difference, like empty space which has neither mixture nor decay. Like the great sun illuminating
the whole world: when the sun rises, brightness spreads everywhere, yet empty space has never become bright; when the sun sets
darkness fills the world, yet empty space has never become dark. The realms of light and dark contend with each other,
but the nature of space remains vast and unchanged. The mind of Buddhas and sentient beings is likewise
the same. If one views Buddha as a form of pure, radiant liberation, and views sentient beings
as forms of defiled darkness and birth and death, then those who hold such views, even through countless kalpas like Ganges sands,
will never attain enlightenment, because they cling to appearances. Only this One Mind exists; there is not even a dust mote
of Dharma to be attained. Mind itself is Buddha. Now students of the Way who do not awaken to this mind
generate mind upon mind, seeking Buddha outwardly. Clinging to appearances and practicing cultivation are all
unwholesome dharmas, not the path of enlightenment. Making offerings to all Buddhas of the ten directions is not equal to making an offering to
a single person of no-mind. Why is this so? Because no-mind means the absence of all minds. The suchness
of its essence is inwardly like wood or stone, unmoving and unshaken; outwardly like empty space, unobstructed and unhindered.
There is no subject and object, no location, no form, no gain or loss. Those who approach it dare not
enter this Dharma, fearing they may fall into emptiness with nowhere to abide, so they look at the cliff and retreat. Thus they all
widely seek knowledge and views. Those who seek knowledge are as many as hairs, while those who awaken to the Way are as rare as horns.
Manjushri represents principle; Samantabhadra represents practice. Principle means the true emptiness that is unobstructed; practice
means the inexhaustible activity free from appearances. Avalokiteshvara represents great compassion, and Mahasthamaprapta represents great wisdom.
Vimalakirti means Pure Name. Purity refers to the nature; name refers to appearance. Nature and appearance
are not different, therefore he is called Pure Name. What the great bodhisattvas symbolize is present in everyone.
Without leaving the One Mind, if one awakens to it, that is it. Yet students of the Way today do not awaken within their own mind,
but cling to appearances outside the mind and grasp at objects; all this turns away from the Way. As for the sands of the Ganges, the Buddha
said they are merely sand. Buddhas, bodhisattvas, Indra, Brahma, and the gods tread upon them,
yet the sand does not rejoice. Cattle, sheep, insects, and ants trample over them, yet the sand does not grow angry.
Treasures and fragrances do not make the sand greedy, and filth and foulness do not make it disgusted. This mind is precisely the mind of no-mind,
free from all appearances. Between sentient beings and Buddhas there is no difference. If one can only
attain no-mind, that is the ultimate. If students of the Way do not directly realize no-mind,
then even practicing through many kalpas they will never accomplish the Way, being bound by the practices of the three vehicles and unable to gain liberation.
However, realization of this mind may be swift or slow. Some, upon hearing the Dharma, in a single thought attain no-mind.
Others reach no-mind only after the ten faiths, ten abodes, ten practices, and ten dedications.
Whether sooner or later, once no-mind is attained one abides there. There is nothing more to cultivate and nothing to realize. In truth nothing is obtained.
This is genuine and not false. Whether obtained in a single thought or attained at the ten stages, the function is exactly
the same, with no difference in depth. One has only vainly undergone toil through many kalpas.
Creating evil or creating good are both attachment to appearances. Creating evil while attached to appearances brings futile rebirth;
creating good while attached to appearances brings futile toil. It is better to recognize the original Dharma immediately upon hearing these words.
This Dharma is mind. Outside mind there is no Dharma. This mind is Dharma. Outside Dharma there is no mind. Mind itself
is originally no-mind, and there is no separate no-mind. If one uses mind to make no-mind, the mind instead becomes something existent. It is only silently in accord.
All thought and deliberation are cut off. Therefore it is said that the path of words is ended and the movement of mind is extinguished.
This mind is the original pure Buddha. Everyone possesses it. All beings that move and all that have life share it equally with
the Buddhas and bodhisattvas, one body without difference. Only because of deluded thoughts and discriminations do they create various karmic
results. Upon the original Buddha there is truly not a single thing. It is empty, open, still, luminous, subtle, and at peace.
Deeply awaken and enter it yourself; directly it is so. It is perfectly complete and lacking nothing.
Even if one diligently cultivates through the three great eons and passes through the various stages,
when realization occurs in a single thought one only realizes that one was originally Buddha. Nothing at all is added beyond this.
Looking back at the efforts of past kalpas, they are all actions in a dream. Therefore the Tathagata said: In regard to unsurpassed
enlightenment I truly obtained nothing. If there had been something obtained, the Buddha Dipankara would not have given me
a prediction. He also said: This Dharma is equal, without high or low; this is called enlightenment.
This original pure mind, together with sentient beings, Buddhas, worlds, mountains, and rivers,
whether with form or without form, pervades the ten directions. All are equal without the mark of self and other. This
original pure mind constantly shines everywhere in perfect clarity. People of the world do not awaken to it; they only take seeing,
hearing, sensing, and knowing to be mind. Being covered by seeing, hearing, sensing, and knowing, they do not perceive the subtle
luminous original body. But if they are directly without mind, the original body appears by itself, like the great sun rising
in empty space, shining everywhere without obstruction. Therefore students of the Way only recognize
seeing, hearing, sensing, knowing, actions, and movements. If seeing, hearing, sensing, and knowing are emptied,
the road of mind is cut off with nowhere to enter. Simply recognize the original mind within seeing, hearing, sensing, and knowing.
Yet the original mind does not belong to seeing, hearing, sensing, and knowing, nor is it separate from them. Only do not
form views upon seeing, hearing, sensing, and knowing, nor let thoughts arise upon them.
Do not seek the mind apart from seeing, hearing, sensing, and knowing, and do not abandon seeing, hearing, sensing, and knowing
to grasp some Dharma. Neither grasp nor reject, neither abide nor cling. Moving freely in all directions, everywhere is the place of the Way.
When people of the world hear that all Buddhas transmit the Dharma of mind, they suppose that within the mind there is some other Dharma
that can be realized and obtained, and so they use mind to seek Dharma, not knowing that mind itself is the Dharma
and Dharma itself is mind. One cannot use mind to seek mind again. Even through thousands of millions of kalpas, in the end
there is never a day of attainment. It is better to be without mind at once; this itself is the original Dharma. It is like a strong man who forgets
the jewel in his forehead and seeks for it outwardly. Though he travels throughout the ten directions, he can never obtain it. A wise person
points it out, and at that moment he himself sees that the original jewel is there as before. Thus students of the Way, deluded about their own
original mind, do not recognize it as Buddha. Therefore they seek outwardly and begin practices and efforts, following stages
of realization. Through countless kalpas of diligent seeking they never accomplish the Way. It is better to be without mind at once, firmly
knowing that all dharmas are originally nothing and there is nothing to attain. With no reliance and no abiding, there is no
subject and no object. If deluded thoughts do not move, enlightenment is realized. When the Way is realized, one only realizes
the Buddha of the original mind. All efforts through kalpas were empty cultivation, just like when the strong man obtains the jewel,
he only obtains the jewel already in his forehead, not because of the strength of searching outwardly. Therefore the Buddha said, I
in regard to unsurpassed enlightenment truly obtained nothing. Fearing that people would not believe this, he therefore cited the five eyes
what is seen by the five eyes and what is spoken by the five kinds of speech are true and not false; this is the ultimate truth.

Students of the Way should not mistake the four great elements for the body. The four elements have no self, and the self has no master.
Therefore know that this body has neither self nor master. The five aggregates are taken as mind, yet the five aggregates have no self
and no master. Therefore know that this mind also has neither self nor master. The six faculties, the six objects, and the six
consciousnesses arise and cease through their conjunction, and it is the same with them. Since the eighteen realms are empty, all is
empty. Only the original mind remains vast and pure. There is food of consciousness and food of wisdom. The body of the four elements
suffers hunger and illness; providing it with nourishment in accord without giving rise to greed or attachment is called food of wisdom.
Indulging desires for flavors and falsely producing discrimination, seeking only what pleases the palate without aversion,
is called food of consciousness. A hearer is one who awakens through sound; therefore he is called a hearer.
Yet he does not understand his own mind and forms interpretations based on teachings heard. Whether through supernatural powers or
auspicious signs, words and actions, hearing of enlightenment, nirvana, and the three immeasurable eons
required to complete the Buddha path, all these belong to the path of the hearer and are called hearer Buddhas. Only directly
awakening to one’s own mind, originally Buddha, with not a single dharma to obtain and not a single practice to cultivate,
this is the unsurpassed Way; this is the Buddha of suchness. Students of the Way only fear that a single
thought arises, and then they are separated from the Way. Thought after thought without form, thought after thought without action, this is
Buddha. If students of the Way wish to become Buddhas, they need not learn all Buddhist teachings.
Only learn not to seek and not to cling. Not seeking means the mind does not arise; not clinging means the mind does not
perish. Not arising and not perishing is Buddha. The eighty-four thousand Dharma gates correspond to the eighty-four
thousand afflictions; they are merely methods of teaching and guiding. In reality there are no dharmas at all. To be free from them
is the Dharma. One who knows this freedom is Buddha. Simply leave all afflictions and there is no Dharma
to be obtained. If students of the Way wish to know the essential key, they must not attach
a single thing to the mind. It is said that the true Dharma body of the Buddha is like empty space. This is a metaphor that the Dharma body is
empty space and empty space is the Dharma body. Ordinary people say the Dharma body pervades space and that space
contains the Dharma body, not knowing that the Dharma body itself is space and space itself is the Dharma body.
If one insists there is space, then space is not the Dharma body. If one insists there is
a Dharma body, the Dharma body is not space. Only do not form a view of space. Space
is the Dharma body. Do not form a view of the Dharma body. The Dharma body is space. Space and the Dharma
body have no different form. Buddha and sentient beings have no different form. Birth and death and nirvana have
no different form. Afflictions and enlightenment have no different form. To be free from all forms is Buddha.
Ordinary people grasp objects; practitioners grasp the mind. When both mind and objects are forgotten, that is the true Dharma.
Forgetting objects is easy, but forgetting the mind is very difficult. People do not dare forget the mind, fearing they will fall into emptiness
with nothing to grasp. They do not know that emptiness is originally not empty; it is only the one true Dharma realm.
This spiritual awareness nature has endured as long as space since beginningless time. It has never been born and never perished.
It has never been existent nor nonexistent, never defiled nor pure, never noisy nor
silent. It has never been young nor old. It has no location, no inside or outside, no measure and no
form. It has no color or shape, no sound. It cannot be sought and cannot be searched for.
It cannot be known by intellectual wisdom, cannot be grasped by words, cannot be understood through objects,
and cannot be reached through effort. All Buddhas and bodhisattvas, together with every living being,
share this great nirvana nature. Nature is mind, mind is Buddha, and Buddha is Dharma.
A single thought departing from truth is delusion. One cannot use mind to seek mind,
nor use Buddha to seek Buddha, nor use Dharma to seek Dharma. Therefore students of the Way
must directly be without mind and silently accord with it. The moment one intends with mind, one deviates. Mind is transmitted by mind;
this is the correct view. Take care not to chase objects outward and take them as mind. That is like recognizing
a thief as one’s own son. Because there is greed, anger, and delusion, precepts, concentration, and wisdom are established. If originally there were no afflictions,
how could there be enlightenment? Therefore the patriarch said: The Buddha taught all dharmas in order to remove all
minds. If I have no mind at all, what use are all dharmas? Upon the original pure Buddha
nothing further can be attached. It is like empty space which, though adorned with immeasurable treasures,
cannot hold them. Buddha nature is like space; though adorned with immeasurable merit and wisdom,
they cannot remain there. It is only because one is deluded about the original nature that it is not seen. The so-called mind
ground Dharma gate means that all dharmas are established upon this mind. When objects are encountered there is existence; without objects
there is none. One must not turn the pure nature into an interpretation of objects. What is called concentration and wisdom,
clear awareness in function, silent yet alert seeing, hearing, sensing, and knowing, are all interpretations made upon objects.
They may be spoken temporarily for people of middling or lesser capacity. But if one wishes to realize personally, none of these views should be made.
All such views belong to object dharmas, appearing and disappearing in the realm of existence. Only when
one makes no view of existence or nonexistence regarding all dharmas does one truly see the Dharma.

On the first day of the ninth month the master said to Xiu: Since the great master Bodhidharma came to China, he has only
taught the One Mind and transmitted only one Dharma. Buddha transmits to Buddha and speaks of no other Buddha. Dharma
transmits Dharma and speaks of no other Dharma. The Dharma is the Dharma that cannot be spoken, and the Buddha is the Buddha that cannot
be grasped. It is the original pure mind. Only this one matter is real; the other
two are not true. Prajna is wisdom, and this wisdom is the formless original mind. Ordinary
people do not turn toward the Way but indulge the six senses and thus travel the six realms. When students of the Way in a single thought
calculate birth and death they fall into the demonic path. When a single thought gives rise to various views they fall into the path of outsiders.
Seeing birth and seeking its extinction, they fall into the path of the hearers. Not seeing birth but seeing only
extinction, they fall into the path of the solitary realizers. The Dharma originally does not arise and now does not cease. Without raising the two
views, neither rejecting nor delighting, all dharmas are only one mind; then it is
the Buddha vehicle. Ordinary people all follow objects and produce mind, and the mind then delights or dislikes. If one wishes
to be without objects, one must forget the mind. When the mind is forgotten, objects become empty; when objects are empty, the mind ceases.
If the mind is not forgotten but objects are merely removed, objects cannot truly be removed and confusion only increases. Therefore all
dharmas are only mind, and mind itself cannot be obtained. What then is there to seek? Those who study prajna do not
see a single dharma to obtain. They cut off intention toward the three vehicles. There is only the one reality which cannot be realized as an attainment.
To say I can realize and obtain it is arrogance. At the assembly of the Lotus Sutra
those who brushed off their robes and left were all such people. Therefore the Buddha said: With regard to enlightenment I truly
obtained nothing; it is only silent accord. When ordinary people approach death, they need only observe that the five aggregates
are all empty and the four elements have no self. The true mind has no form and neither goes nor comes. When one is born the nature
does not come; when one dies the nature does not go. It remains clear and perfectly still, with mind and objects as one.
If one can be like this, directly and suddenly realizing it, one will not be bound by the three times and will be
a person beyond the world. One must not have the slightest inclination. If good signs appear and various Buddhas
come to welcome you or other visions appear, have no mind to follow them. If evil signs
appear in many forms, also have no mind of fear. Simply forget the mind itself and be the same as the Dharma realm.
Then one attains freedom. This is the essential point.

On the eighth day of the tenth month the master said to Xiu: The so-called phantom city refers to the two vehicles and the ten stages,
equal enlightenment and wondrous enlightenment. All are provisional teachings established to guide and attract; they are the phantom city.
The treasure place is the treasure of the true mind, the original Buddha nature. This treasure does not belong to conceptual measure.
It cannot be established. There is no Buddha and no sentient beings, no subject and no object. Where then
would there be a city? If one asks where the treasure place is, since this is the phantom city, the treasure place cannot
be pointed out. To point is to give a location, and that would not be the true treasure place. Therefore it is said to be simply near.
It cannot be fixed by description. One must personally realize and accord with it. The term icchantika
means lacking faith. All beings in the six realms and even those of the two vehicles who do not believe
in the Buddha fruit are called icchantikas who have cut off good roots. A bodhisattva is one who deeply believes
in the Buddha Dharma and does not see a great vehicle or small vehicle, seeing Buddha and sentient beings as the same Dharma nature.
Such a one is called an icchantika of good roots. Generally, those who awaken through verbal teachings are called
hearers. Those who awaken by contemplating causes and conditions are called solitary realizers. If one does not awaken within one’s own mind,
then even if one reaches Buddhahood it is called a hearer Buddha. Students of the Way often awaken through
doctrinal teachings but not through the Dharma of mind. Though they cultivate through many kalpas, they will
never be the original Buddha. If one does not awaken in the mind but only through teachings,
one belittles mind and values doctrine, chasing fragments and forgetting the original mind. Therefore only accord with the original mind
and do not seek Dharma. Mind itself is Dharma. Ordinary people are often obstructed in mind by objects and in principle by affairs.
They often wish to escape objects to calm the mind and avoid affairs to preserve principle, not knowing that it is
the mind that obstructs objects and principle that obstructs affairs. If the mind is empty, objects become empty; if principle is silent,
affairs themselves become still. Do not use the mind in reverse. Most people are unwilling to empty the mind, fearing they will fall
into emptiness. They do not know their own mind is originally empty. The foolish remove affairs but not the mind; the wise
remove the mind but not affairs. The mind of a bodhisattva is like space, abandoning everything.
All meritorious deeds are done without attachment. There are three kinds of relinquishment. To relinquish inward and outward body and mind entirely,
like space with nothing grasped or clung to, and then respond to things in accord with circumstances, with
subject and object both forgotten, this is great relinquishment. If one practices the Way and gives merit while at the same time
relinquishing them without hope for reward, this is middle relinquishment. If one widely cultivates good deeds while having some expectation,
but upon hearing the Dharma understands emptiness and therefore does not cling, this is small relinquishment. Great relinquishment is like
a torch before one, with no more confusion or awakening. Middle relinquishment is like a torch at one’s side, sometimes bright
and sometimes dim. Small relinquishment is like a torch behind one, unable to see the pits ahead. Therefore the bodhisattva’s mind
is like space, relinquishing everything. The past mind cannot be obtained; this is relinquishment of the past.
The present mind cannot be obtained; this is relinquishment of the present. The future mind cannot be obtained; this is
relinquishment of the future. This is called relinquishing the three times. Since the Tathagata transmitted the Dharma to Kashyapa,
it has been mind sealing mind, mind to mind without difference. If the seal is placed on emptiness, no mark is made.
If the seal is placed on a thing, it does not form the Dharma. Therefore mind seals mind and mind and mind are not different.
Both the sealer and the sealed are difficult to realize; therefore few attain it. Yet mind itself is no mind.
Attainment itself is no attainment. The Buddha has three bodies. The Dharma body teaches the Dharma of empty and open self nature. The reward
body teaches all pure dharmas. The transformation body teaches the six perfections and myriad practices. The Dharma body
teaches the Dharma that cannot be sought through words, sounds, forms, or written characters.
There is nothing spoken and nothing realized; it is simply the empty openness of self nature. Therefore it is said that having no Dharma to speak is
called teaching the Dharma. The reward body and transformation body appear according to circumstances, and the Dharma they teach also follows
events and capacities to guide beings. All these are not the true Dharma. Therefore it is said that the reward and transformation are not the true
Buddha, nor are they the true teacher of the Dharma. What is spoken of as one bright essence dividing into six harmonies
means that the one bright essence is the one mind, and the six harmonies are the six faculties.
Each of the six faculties joins with its object: the eye with form, the ear with sound, the nose with scent,
the tongue with taste, the body with touch, and the mind with dharmas. Between them arise the six
consciousnesses, forming the eighteen realms. If one understands that the eighteen realms have no reality, the six harmonies
are gathered back into the one bright essence, which is mind. Students of the Way all know this,
yet they cannot avoid making interpretations of one essence and six harmonies, and thus they are bound by dharmas
and fail to accord with the original mind. When the Tathagata appeared in the world, if he wished to teach the one vehicle true Dharma, beings would not
believe it and would slander it, sinking into the sea of suffering. If he said nothing at all, he would fall into stinginess and fail to
generously bestow the wondrous Way upon beings. Therefore he established skillful means and taught three vehicles.
The vehicles have great and small, and realization has shallow and deep, yet none are the original Dharma. Therefore it is said that only the one vehicle Way exists and the other
two are not true. Yet even then the Dharma of the One Mind was not fully revealed. Therefore he called Kashyapa to the same
Dharma seat and separately transmitted the One Mind, the Dharma beyond words. This single branch of the teaching was made to proceed separately.
Whoever can accord with and awaken to it will reach the Buddha stage.

Someone asked: What is the Way and how is it practiced? The master said: What thing is the Way that you wish
to practice? They asked: How do the teachers everywhere who transmit the tradition practice meditation and study the Way? The master said:
Those are words for guiding people of dull capacity and cannot be relied upon.
They asked: If that is for dull capacities, what Dharma is spoken for those of superior capacity? The master said:
If one is of superior capacity, why seek it from others? One cannot even obtain oneself,
so how could there be another Dharma that satisfies the mind? Do you not see where the teachings say: What is the form of each dharma?
They said: If so, then there is nothing at all to seek. The master said: If that is the case,
it saves effort. They said: But then it becomes complete annihilation; surely it is not nothing.
The master said: Who told you it is nothing? Who is that it? You are trying to seek it.
They said: If seeking is not allowed, why also say not to cut it off? The master said: If you do not seek,
then stop. Who tells you to cut it off? When you see the empty space before you, how would you cut
it off? They said: If this Dharma can be obtained, is it the same as space? The master said: Space sooner or later
will tell you whether there is sameness or difference. I only speak like this for the moment, yet you immediately
produce interpretations from it. They said: Should one then not allow people to form interpretations? The master said: I have never
obstructed you. But interpretation belongs to emotion; when emotion arises, wisdom is separated.
here should not give rise to feelings, is that so. The master said. If no feeling arises, who then says it is so.

A monk asked. Whenever one speaks to the master, why does the master say the words fall into error.
The master said. You simply do not understand speech. What falling into error is there.

A monk asked. All the many words spoken earlier were merely opposing words. There has never been
any real Dharma pointed out to people. The master said. The real Dharma has no inversion. In the place where you now question
inversion arises by itself. What real Dharma are you seeking. The monk said. Since inversion arises in the questioning itself,
then how about the master’s answer. The master said. Just take something and look at your own face. Do not
concern yourself with others. He also said. It is like a foolish dog that
barks wherever it sees something move. When wind moves grass and trees it cannot tell the difference. He also said. In this Chan school of ours, since ancient times
it has never taught people to seek knowledge or understanding. We only say to study the Way, which already
is merely a phrase for guiding beginners. Yet the Way cannot truly be studied. If feelings remain that seek learning and understanding, it instead becomes
a delusion of the Way. The Way has no location and is called the mind of the Great Vehicle. This mind is not within, without, or in between.
In truth it has no location. Above all do not make intellectual interpretations. It is only said because your present
measuring mind operates through feelings. If such measuring ends, the mind has no location. This Way is naturally so and originally
has no name. Only because people do not recognize it and are lost in feelings do the Buddhas
appear in the world and explain this matter. Fearing that you people would not understand, they provisionally establish the name Way. But
you must not cling to the name and produce interpretations. Therefore it is said. Once the fish is caught the trap is forgotten. Body and mind then naturally realize
the Way and recognize the mind. Realizing the source is therefore called a renunciant. The fruit of a renunciant is to
cease thoughts and accomplish it, not to obtain it through study. Now you use mind to seek mind, going to other people's houses
merely trying to learn and obtain something. When will you ever gain it. In ancient times people had sharp faculties and upon hearing a single word
they immediately ended learning. Therefore they were called people of no-learning, idle practitioners of non-action. Nowadays
people only wish for much knowledge and understanding. They widely seek scriptural meanings and call this practice.
They do not know that much knowledge and interpretation instead become obstruction. It is like giving children much cream and milk
to eat without knowing whether it is digested or not. Students of the three vehicles are all like this.
They are all called those who cannot digest their food. So-called undigested knowledge and interpretations are all poison.
They all grasp within birth and death. Within true suchness there is no such thing. Therefore it is said. In my
royal treasury there is no such sword. All previous understandings must
be entirely set aside and made empty, with no further discrimination. This is the empty treasury of the Tathagata.
In the treasury of the Tathagata there is not even the smallest dust. This is the Dharma king who destroys existence appearing in the world.
It is also said. From Dipankara Buddha I obtained not the slightest Dharma. These words only
serve to empty your conceptual understanding. When inside and outside are melted away and feelings are exhausted,
with no attachment remaining, one becomes a person with nothing to do. The doctrinal nets of the three vehicles are merely medicines suited to capacities.
They are temporary teachings given according to circumstances, each different. If one understands this,
one will not be deluded. Above all do not cling to words in any single teaching or method. Why is this so.
Because in truth there is no fixed Dharma that the Tathagata can speak. In this school of ours
we do not discuss such matters. Only know that when the mind ceases it is finished. There is no need to think before or after.

A monk asked. It is often said that mind itself is Buddha. Which mind exactly is Buddha.
The master said. How many minds do you have. The monk said. Is the ordinary mind Buddha, or
is the holy mind Buddha. The master said. Where do you have ordinary or holy mind.
The monk said. The three vehicles speak of ordinary and holy. How can the master say there is none.
The master said. In the three vehicles it clearly tells you that the mind of ordinary and holy is delusion. You do not understand and instead cling
to it as existing, taking emptiness as reality. Is this not delusion. Because of delusion the mind becomes confused. You need only remove
ordinary feelings and holy realms. Outside the mind there is no other Buddha. When the patriarch came from the west he directly pointed out
that all people entirely are Buddha. Yet you do not recognize this, clinging to ordinary and holy and running outward.
In doing so you only confuse your own mind. Therefore it is said to you that mind itself is Buddha. When a single thought
of attachment arises you fall into other paths. From beginningless time until today it has never been different.
Since there is no different Dharma it is called perfect enlightenment. The monk asked. What principle does the master mean by the word is.
The master said. What principle are you seeking. The moment there is a principle the mind is already different.
The monk said. Earlier you said from beginningless time it is not different from today. What is that principle.
The master said. Because you seek, you make it different yourself. If you did not seek, where would difference be.
The monk said. If there is no difference, why use the word is. The master said. If you did not believe in ordinary and holy,
who would say is to you. If it neither is nor is not, the mind is also not mind. When mind and is
are both forgotten, where would you go to seek.

A monk asked. Delusion obstructs the true mind. How should it now be dispelled. The master said. If you raise
delusion to eliminate delusion, that too becomes delusion. Delusion originally has no root. It only exists because of discrimination.
If feelings toward both ordinary and holy are exhausted, delusion naturally disappears. If you still think about how to eliminate it,
you must not have the slightest attachment. This is called letting both arms fall away and you will surely
obtain Buddhahood. The monk said. If there is no attachment, how is it transmitted. The master said. Mind transmits mind.
The monk said. If mind transmits mind, why say that mind also does not exist.
The master said. Not obtaining a single Dharma is called transmission of mind. If this mind is understood, it is no mind and no Dharma.
The monk said. If there is no mind and no Dharma, how is it called transmission.
The master said. When you hear transmission of mind you suppose something is obtained. Therefore the patriarch said that when the nature of mind is recognized it may be said to be inconceivable.
It is clearly without attainment. When it is attained it is not spoken of as known. If this matter were made for you
to understand conceptually, it would not be possible.

A monk asked. The empty space before us is an object, is it not. Is there not a way to point to the object and see the mind.
The master said. What mind tells you to see it in objects. Even if you see it,
it is only a mind reflecting objects. It is like a person using a mirror to look at his face.
Even if the eyebrows and eyes are clearly seen, they are merely reflections. What does that have to do with you.
The monk said. Without reflection, how could one ever see. The master said. If it depends on causes, it must always rely on things.
When will it ever end. Did you not hear it said to you. Let go like a person with nothing in hand,
and all the many explanations are useless. The monk said. If one truly understands, is even reflection unnecessary.
The master said. If there is nothing, what need is there for reflection. Do not open your eyes
and speak recklessly.

In a sermon the master said. A hundred kinds of knowledge are inferior to having no seeking. This is foremost. A practitioner
is a person with nothing to do. There truly are not so many kinds of mind, nor any principles to speak of. Nothing
more to say. Disperse.

A monk asked. What is worldly truth. The master said. Why speak tangled words. Originally it is pure.
Why rely on words and answers. Simply having no mind at all is called undefiled wisdom.
In your daily walking, standing, sitting, lying down, and all speech, simply do not cling to conditioned dharmas.
Every word and blink of the eye can accord with the undefiled. In this latter age
many who study Chan cling to sounds and forms. Why not let your mind be like empty space.
Become like dry wood and stones, like cold ashes and a dead fire. Only then will there be some
small correspondence. If not, one day Yama will interrogate you.
Just leave behind all dharmas of existence and nonexistence. Let the mind be like the sun always in empty space.
Its light naturally shines without deliberate shining. This is not something accomplished by saving effort. When you reach this
point there is no place to rest. This is practicing the conduct of all Buddhas and means giving rise to mind without abiding anywhere.
This is your pure Dharma body and is called unsurpassed enlightenment.
If you do not understand this meaning, even if you learn much and practice diligently, wearing grass robes
and eating roots, if you do not know your own mind it is all deviant practice and you will become followers of demons.
What benefit is there in such practice. Master Zhigong said that Buddha is originally made by one's own mind.
How could it be sought in written words. Even if you learn the stages of the three sages, four fruits, and ten bhumis
filling your mind, you still sit within ordinary and holy. Have you not heard that all conditioned things are impermanent.
They are dharmas of birth and death. When their force is exhausted the arrow falls again and brings an unsatisfactory next life.
How could that compare with the gate of the unconditioned true reality, where with one leap one enters the ground of the Tathagata.
Since you are not yet such a person, you must study broadly the teachings of the ancients.
Zhigong said that without meeting an enlightened teacher one takes the medicine of the Great Vehicle in vain.
Now at all times in walking, standing, sitting, and lying down, simply learn no mind. After a long time it will truly
be realized. Because your strength is small you cannot leap across at once. But in three, five, or
ten years you must gain an entry point and naturally understand.
Because you cannot do so, you must use mind to study Chan and the Way. What connection does this have with the Buddha Dharma.
Therefore it is said that what the Tathagata taught was all for guiding people, like using yellow leaves as gold to stop a child from crying.
It is certainly not real. If something real were obtained, that person would not belong to this school.
What connection would it have with your own nature. Therefore the scripture says that truly there is not the slightest Dharma to obtain.
This is called unsurpassed enlightenment. If you understand this meaning you will know that the Buddha path
and the demon path are both mistaken. Originally it is bright and pure, with no square or round, no large or small,
no long or short. There is no defilement, no action, no delusion, no enlightenment. Clearly seen, there is
not a single thing. There is neither person nor Buddha. The worlds of the great cosmos are like bubbles in the sea.
All sages and worthies are like flashes of lightning. Nothing equals the true Dharma body of the mind.
From ancient times until now it is the same as the Buddhas and patriarchs. Nowhere is even a hair lacking.
Having understood this meaning you must strive in this life, for the outgoing breath does not guarantee the incoming one.

A monk asked. The sixth patriarch did not know the scriptures, so how did he receive the robe and become patriarch. Elder Shenxiu
was head monk among five hundred and a teaching master who lectured on thirty two scriptures and treatises.
Why was the robe not transmitted to him. The master said. Because he had a mind of action and cultivated
and realized something he regarded as real. Therefore the fifth patriarch transmitted to the sixth.
The sixth patriarch simply silently accorded and received the profound meaning of the Tathagata.
That is why the Dharma was transmitted to him. Have you not heard that the original Dharma is no Dharma and even no Dharma is Dharma.
When transmitting no Dharma, what Dharma is truly Dharma. If you understand this meaning you may truly be called a renunciant.
Then you may properly practice. If you do not believe this, consider how the monk Ming ran to Dayu Ridge
to seek the sixth patriarch. The sixth patriarch asked him whether he came for the robe or for the Dharma.
Ming replied that he came not for the robe but for the Dharma.
The sixth patriarch told him to gather his thoughts for a moment and think neither of good nor evil.
When Ming obeyed, the patriarch said that at the moment of thinking neither good nor evil,
bring forth the face you had before your parents were born.
At these words Ming suddenly understood and bowed, saying that it was like a person drinking water who knows for himself whether it is warm or cold.
He said that for thirty years in the assembly of the fifth patriarch he had labored in vain.
Only today did he realize his former error. The sixth patriarch said that only at this moment does one know
that the patriarch coming from the west directly pointed to the human mind so that seeing one’s nature one becomes Buddha, not through words.
Ananda once asked Mahakasyapa what the World Honored One transmitted besides the golden robe.
Mahakasyapa called Ananda and Ananda answered. Mahakasyapa then told him to knock down the flagpole in front of the gate.
This was the sign of the patriarchs. For thirty years Ananda served as attendant,
yet because of his great learning the Buddha reproached him saying that a thousand days of studying wisdom
are not equal to one day of studying the Way. Without studying the Way even a drop of water is hard to digest.

A monk asked how to avoid falling into stages. The master said to eat all day without biting
a single grain of rice, to walk all day without stepping on a single piece of ground.
At such times there are no marks of self or others. All day one does not leave the activities of the world yet is not deluded by objects.
Only then is one called a free person. At all times and in every thought see no forms.
Do not grasp the past, present, and future. The past does not depart, the present does not abide, the future does not arrive.
Sit peacefully and let things follow their course without constraint. This is called liberation. Strive diligently.
Among thousands in this gate only three or five attain it. If you do not take it seriously,
the day of suffering will surely come. Therefore it is said that one must resolve it in this life.
Who could endure the remaining calamities for countless kalpas.

cb:juan.cb:jhead: End of The Essentials of the Transmission of Mind by the Chan Master of Huangbo Mountain

app.rdg: 黃檗山斷際禪師傳心法要

app.lem: 黃檗山斷際禪師傳心法要

app.rdg: 者有至十地乃得無心者

p.note: 斷際心要【大】，黃檗山斷際禪師傳心法要【甲】，【原】增上寺報恩藏明本，【甲】寬文十三年刊宗教大學藏本
者【大】，者有至十地乃得無心者【甲】
情量知解【大】，情解知量【甲】
【原】增上寺報恩藏明本，【甲】寬文十三年刊宗教大學藏本，斷際心要＝黃檗山斷際禪師傳心法要【甲】
者＋（有至十地乃得無心者）【甲】
【原】增上寺報恩藏明本，【甲】寬文十三年刊宗教大學藏本
